subject: hindi
title: Half the students speak Bhojpuri at home in a Hindi-medium school
query_text: Half the students speak Bhojpuri at home in a Hindi-medium school

immediate_steps.title: Immediate Action
steps: Welcome Bhojpuri words in class — when a child says something in Bhojpuri, smile and say 'हाँ, हमारे घर में भी ऐसे बोलते हैं' — this builds trust instantly
Start each new Hindi word by first asking 'Bhojpuri mein isko kya kehte hain?' — then bridge to the Hindi word together as a class
Pair Bhojpuri-speaking students with Hindi-dominant students for oral activities — both learn from each other naturally

classroom_activity.title: Classroom Activity — 'Do Bhasha, Ek Baat' (Two Languages, One Meaning)
classroom_activity.activity: Write a simple Hindi word on the blackboard (e.g., 'पानी'). Ask Bhojpuri-speaking children what they call it at home ('पनिया'). Write both side by side. Ask: 'Dono ka matlab ek hi hai?' Children nod and connect. Do this with 5 words — roti, ghar, maa, khana, neend. Children feel their home language is valued, and Hindi vocabulary gets anchored to something familiar.

inclusion_strategy.title: Inclusion Strategy
inclusion_strategy.strategy: For children struggling with Hindi reading, allow them to first narrate the story or answer in Bhojpuri orally — then help them find the Hindi words for what they just said. This bilingual bridge approach ensures comprehension is not blocked by language anxiety, keeping all learners participating actively.

reflection_prompt.title: Reflection
reflection_prompt.question: After trying the 'Do Bhasha' activity, which children seemed more confident to speak up — and did any surprise you?

source_citation.title: Source
source_citation.reference: NEP 2020, Section 4.12 — Multilingualism and home language use in early grades; NEP 2020, Section 4.11 — Bilingual teaching-learning approach where home language differs from medium of instruction
